sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Nine — slope and rate of change. If you've ever climbed a steep staircase versus a gentle one, you already feel slope. Today we put a real number on that feeling.

Look at any wheelchair ramp or roof. Two roofs can rise the same total height, but the steeper one packs that rise into a shorter horizontal stretch. That ratio — rise per run — is the slope. Engineers use the exact same number we are about to learn.

Game plan. First, slope from a graph — rise over run, counted right off the grid. Second, slope from two points using one formula. Third, the four kinds of slope. Fourth, slope as rate of change in real life. By the end you will read any line the way you read a sentence.

Rise over run, straight from a graph. Pick any two clean grid points the line passes through. Rise is how far you go up or down between them. Run is how far you go right. Slope equals rise divided by run. The grid is doing the arithmetic for you — you are just counting squares.

Now without a graph, just two points. Slope, written m, equals y two minus y one over x two minus x one. That formula is not magic — the top is the rise, the bottom is the run. Try the points one comma two and four comma eight. Eight minus two is six, four minus one is three, six over three is two. Slope is two.

Here is the trap that costs students the most points. The order has to match. Whichever point you call point one, use its y on top and its x on bottom. If you subtract y two minus y one but then do x one minus x two, you flip the sign and get the wrong direction. Also — y always goes on top. Mix x and y and the answer is just nonsense.
Your turn. Find the slope between the points two comma three and five comma twelve. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Twelve minus three is nine. Five minus two is three. Nine over three is three. The slope is three. For every one step right, the line climbs three. If you got negative three, you reversed only one of the subtractions — that is exactly the trap we just named.

Four flavors of slope, and you have to keep them apart. Positive slope — line goes uphill left to right. Negative slope — line goes downhill. Zero slope — flat line, no rise at all, the number is literally zero. Undefined slope — vertical line, no run, and dividing by zero is illegal, so we do not call it zero, we call it undefined. Zero and undefined are not the same thing.

Rate of change is just slope wearing street clothes. Driving sixty kilometers per hour — that is a slope, kilometers per hour. Saving five hundred dollars a month — slope, dollars per month. A road sign that says eight percent grade — slope of zero point zero eight. Anytime you hear per, you are hearing slope.
Real-world one. A water tank starts at fifty liters. Six hours later it has thirty two liters. Find the rate of change in liters per hour. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Treat time as x and water as y. The points are zero comma fifty and six comma thirty two. Thirty two minus fifty is negative eighteen. Six minus zero is six. Negative eighteen over six is negative three. The tank is losing three liters per hour. Negative slope makes sense — the water is going down.

Recap. Slope is rise over run. From two points, m equals y two minus y one over x two minus x one — same numerator, same denominator order. Positive uphill, negative downhill, zero flat, vertical undefined. Rate of change is the same idea, just with units like dollars per month or liters per hour.

Reminder — this video is the overview. To actually master slope, do the rest. Read OpenStax Chapter three on slope of a line. Run the Khan Academy practice set on slope from two points and from a graph. Do the assignment in your Learn Portal dashboard, then book an advisor check-in if any pause problem felt shaky. Next up, Module Ten — using slope to write line equations.
